channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Olivia Wilde Builds Comic Tension in ‘The Invite’
item.description: The director and actress discusses a tense scene from her dinner-party comedy, in which she stars with Seth Rogen, Penélope Cruz and Edward Norton.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: Review: ‘Suddenly Last Summer’ Opera Premieres at Bard
item.description: Tennessee Williams’s darkly operatic one-act play becomes a proper opera in a new adaptation by the composer Courtney Bryan.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: Fisher, Richard B, Center for the Performing Arts at Bard College

item.title: ‘The Chosen’ Studio Is Accused of Shortchanging Crowdfunders
item.description: A lawsuit by a former shareholder says he was not fairly compensated when the company behind the popular drama about Jesus’ life went private.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez

item.title: Bayeux Tapestry Arrives in U.K. After Maximum-Security Operation
item.description: The 11th-century tapestry will go on display at the British Museum in September. Tickets for the exhibition have already been selling rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The tapestry in Bayeux, France, in 2018. British museums and officials have repeatedly asked to borrow it.

item.title: CeCe Winans Revisits Pared-Down Worship Music on ‘The Hymns’
item.description: Best known for contemporary stylings of praise music that topped charts across genres, this singer’s new album of pared-down sacred music, “The Hymns,” put her back atop the gospel charts.
item.dc:creator: Robert M. Marovich
item.media:description: CeCe Winans’s “The Hymns” debuted at No. 1 on Billboard’s Top Gospel Albums chart, her 12th LP to do so.

item.title: Dinaw Mengestu Resigns as PEN America President After 7 Months in Role
item.description: The novelist Dinaw Mengestu, who was elected seven months ago, said the article “continues this approach toward defending some rights while not defending others.”
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:description: Dinaw Mengestu had been on PEN America’s board for about a decade before he became its president in December.

item.title: Jay-Z’s Definitive Album? Debating ‘Reasonable Doubt’ vs. ‘The Blueprint’
item.description: Ahead of the rapper’s three celebratory nights at Yankee Stadium, our “Popcast” hosts debate two major Jay-Z albums.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Joe Coscarelli

item.title: Now Entering Noir City
item.description: Crime and mystery fans can catch a 10-day festival of film noir at the Paris Theater, or find some killer movies streaming on major platforms.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.media:description: Rita Johnson and Ray Milland in a still from “The Big Clock” (1948).

item.title: In ‘Remake,’ a Life Documented on Film Prompts Painful Questions
item.description: Ross McElwee (“Sherman’s March”) reconsiders footage of himself and his family, including a son who died of an overdose.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: In the Pathologic Game Series, Failure Is Inevitable
item.description: In the psychological horror series Pathologic, eerie theatrics and impossible decisions unfold in a Russian town overrun by plague.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Byrd

item.title: Michelle Buteau and the Surprising Transgression of “Survival of the Thickest”
item.description: The Netflix rom-com series that just ended after three seasons succeeded thanks to an irresistible performance by its star, Michelle Buteau.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:description: Michelle Buteau in the third season of Netflix’s “Survival of the Thickest.”

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include two 2026 animated adventures and the finale to a Guillermo del Toro franchise.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman

item.title: ‘The Ghost in the Shell’ Director Wanted Humans in Charge
item.description: In an interview, Mokochan discusses his new adaptation of the classic cyberpunk manga and why this story continues to resonate.
item.dc:creator: Rafael Motamayor
item.category: The Ghost in the Shell (TV Program)

item.title: The Best Seat in the House Is No Seat at All
item.description: Multimedia art is often challenging, provoking. Now it is also potentially bruising. Check your joints before you check out these summer shows.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Lexi Parra

item.title: ‘Giulia’ Review: Jennifer Nettles Delivers Empowerment (and Poison)
item.description: A stirring but tonally muddled new musical about the “poison queen of Palermo” gets an elegant Off Broadway production.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Perelman, Ronald O, Performing Arts Center
Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo (Play)
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jennifer Nettles as the title character in “Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo” at the Perelman Performing Arts Center in Manhattan.

item.title: Historian Kerri K. Greenidge and Her Book ‘The Grimkes’ Come Under Fire
item.description: Kerri K. Greenidge appeared to lose her professorship at Tufts University after scholars began scrutinizing her 2022 book, “The Grimkes,” which is no longer listed on its publisher’s website.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Black Radical: The Life and Times of William Monroe Trotter (Book)
item.media:credit: Mark Elzey Jr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Historians are questioning the validity of multiple works by the historian Kerri K. Greenidge, who is no longer employed by Tufts University.

item.title: Museum Spreads 800 Pounds of Peanut Butter in Tribute to Dutch Artist
item.description: An installation honoring the artist, Wim T. Schippers, features a 270-square-foot hexagon spread on the floor of a museum in Rotterdam.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Workers at the Museum Boijmans Van Beuningen in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, recreated “Peanut Butter Floor,” an art installation by Wim T. Schippers, last week.

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Upgrade your movie night with these picks, featuring karate-chopping families, rogue assassins and more.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: 7 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass (Movie)

item.title: Hershel Parker, Melville Scholar of Ahab-Like Obsessiveness, Dies at 90
item.description: The author of an exhaustive two-volume biography, he was “fanatical about his approach to scholarship and infatuated with Melville,” a colleague said.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Vast Horizon,’ by Anna Thomasson
item.description: A group of artists gathered at a hotel on the Côte d’Azur in 1937. A new book by Anna Thomasson captures the art and escapades the holiday inspired.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin
item.category: A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
item.media:description: Pablo Picasso and Dora Maar at Golfe-Juan, France, on the Côte d’Azur in 1937.

item.title: How Olivia Wilde Directed an Awkward Dance in ‘The Invite’
item.description: The director and actress Olivia Wilde breaks down a tension-filled scene from her new film “The Invite” for Anatomy of a Scene.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy, Gabriel Blanco, Haimy Assefa, Léo Hamelin, Sutton Raphael, Laura Salaberry and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: Ronny Chieng Tackles Trump's Two Air Force Ones
item.description: “Is that why there’s no gas in the world anymore? Because the president flies with an extra emotional support plane?” Chieng said on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in July
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Travis Diehl covers the ramblings of Tinmantis, Jill Magid’s politics, a gory group show and Erin Johnson’s look at Lawrence, Kan., after “The Day After.”
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl

item.title: Wes Anderson and Luke Wilson Walk Into an Elevator and … Get Stuck
item.description: Don’t worry, they’re fine, though the Los Angeles Fire Department did have to use a hand crank to free them.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Richard Harbaugh/Academy Museum Foundation, via Getty Images
item.media:description: From left, Wilson, Anderson and Brooks at the Academy Museum of Motion Pictures in Los Angeles on Monday for a 30th anniversary celebration of Anderson’s film “Bottle Rocket.” The group got stuck when they tried to leave the event.

item.title: Lonnie Bunch Defends Smithsonian Museum After Negative White House Report
item.description: In a letter to staff, Lonnie G. Bunch III wrote that the report was “not a fair characterization of the work and totality of the National Museum of American History.”
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:description: Secretary Lonnie G. Bunch III has tried not to antagonize President Trump while holding fast to what he says is the Smithsonian’s mission to present a full account of American history.

item.title: Bonnie Tyler, Who Sang ‘Total Eclipse of the Heart,’ Dies at 75
item.description: With a voice both weathered and operatic, she minted 1980s pop anthems like “Total Eclipse” and “Holding Out for a Hero.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Adrian Greer Michael Short/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bonnie Tyler in 1983. Her biggest hit, “Total Eclipse of the Heart,” became a cultural mainstay that has 1.1 billion streams on Spotify and 1.3 billion views on YouTube.

item.title: 5 Classical Music Albums You Can Listen to Right Now
item.description: Mozart choral works, a pairing of Dvorak and Carlos Simon and piano music by Philip Glass are among our selections.

item.title: Paul Thek’s Art Legacy: A Shriek and a Giggle
item.description: A pioneering painter, sculptor and installation artist, he achieved posthumous glory as a rule breaker, as two riveting exhibitions show.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: ‘The Man Will Burn’: On Filming the Sacred and Propane at Burning Man
item.description: The directors of a new four-part docuseries talked about why the long-running desert festival is important — and like being on “another planet.”
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: Julia Weist Was Investigated for Making Art. ‘Questioning,’ a New Play, Tells the Story.
item.description: A new play is its own piece of art: A first-person account of an official inquiry into an artist’s use of private investigation databases to create work.
item.dc:creator: Carolina A. Miranda
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: The artist Julia Weist at her studio in Catskill, N.Y.

item.title: ‘Westhampton’ Review: A Humiliating Return
item.description: They say that you can’t go home again; in this movie, a young filmmaker learns that the hard way.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘Night Nurse’ Review: An After-Hours, Erotic Con
item.description: In this thriller, a phone scam is being run out of an elder care facility. Eleni, a skittish but observant nurse, quickly becomes enmeshed with two others in the grift.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Reading Lolita in Tehran’ Review: Seeing the Words Clearly
item.description: A film adaptation of Azar Nafisi’s celebrated memoir of teaching literature in a repressive Iran suggests that the story might be more suited to the page.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Moana’ Review: It Doesn’t Go Far
item.description: This live-action remake of the 2016 animated film has nothing to add to the original, and winds up subtracting instead.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass’ Review: Hamming It Up
item.description: A small-town naïf heads to Los Angeles to bed her celebrity crush (Jon Hamm) in this bonkers sex comedy.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis
item.category: Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass (Movie)

item.title: ‘Do You Love Me’ Review: Images of a Beirut Beyond War
item.description: The director Lana Daher creates a complex emotional portrait of Lebanon with found footage assembled into a 75-minute film of memory, trauma and life.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Evil Dead Burn’ Review: Stop, Drop and Kill
item.description: A dark spirit boils and sears its way through an unhappy family in the latest “Evil Dead” installment.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Barrio Triste’ Review: Bad Boys With a Movie Camera
item.description: This experimental and earnest film from the Colombian American photographer Stillz follows a group of wayward boys in 1980s Medellín.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: Review: ‘Little House on the Prairie’ Adds On an Expansion
item.description: The classic story (and recent culture-war object) remains wholesome but makes room for people who called the prairie home before the Ingalls family.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)

item.title: Which Version of the ‘Odyssey’ Should You Read?
item.description: Homer’s “Odyssey” has been translated into English countless times, with versions ranging from contemporary and accessible to highly poetic. A.O. Scott, critic at large for The New York Times Book Review, breaks down three translations and explains which one might be right for you.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott, Léo Hamelin, Gabriel Blanco, Laura Salaberry and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: Historical Fiction Books That Will Transport You to the 1960s
item.description: The best-selling author Kerri Maher recommends books that transport readers to the decade of space travel, counterculture and tumultuous societal change.
item.dc:creator: Kerri Maher
item.media:credit: Image by The New York Times; Author photo by Kate Eden Renyi Photography

item.title: Late Night Joins the Hunt for Mitch McConnell
item.description: “Apparently, people don’t like a majority senator disappearing with no explanation for three weeks,” Ronny Chieng said on Wednesday’s “Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Stressed desserts, L.A. Metro music and pinball everywhere.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Taylor Swift and Reality TV Shows Like ‘Summer House’ Earn Emmy Nods
item.description: Nominations for unscripted programs recognized a certain newlywed and the scandalous reality hit of the spring.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Justin Bieber and Burna Boy Join Inaugural World Cup Halftime Show
item.description: The artists will perform with Madonna, Shakira and BTS in the first-ever halftime show for a World Cup final. The aim is to replicate the cultural sway of the Super Bowl.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: Justin Bieber, a two-time Grammy Award winner known for hits like “Baby” and “Never Say Never,” performing at the Coachella music festival in April.

item.title: Emmys Snubs and Surprises 2026: Jon Hamm, Ariana Madix and More
item.description: Other unexpected results include nods for Chase Infiniti and the “Love Island USA” host Ariana Madix and a snub for “The Amazing Race.”
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:credit: Jessica Kourkounis/Apple TV+, via Associated Press
item.media:description: “Your Friends & Neighbors,” the Apple TV suburban satire, was a surprise nominee for best drama, but the star, Jon Hamm, was snubbed.

item.title: ‘Love Story,’ FX’s JFK Jr. Series, Picks Up Fewer Emmy Nods Than Expected
item.description: The FX docudrama about the tragic romance between John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette received fewer nominations than its competitors.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: Love Story: John F Kennedy Jr & Carolyn Bessette (TV Program)

item.title: Trump’s Name Must Remain Off Kennedy Center For Now, Appeals Court Rules
item.description: The Trump administration is challenging a federal judge’s ruling that only Congress has the power to rebrand the Washington arts institution.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Before the Kennedy Center removed President Trump’s name from the building’s facade, workers erected scaffolding covered in tarps, obscuring the work.

item.title: Where to Stream 2026 Emmy-Nominated Series: ‘The Pitt,’ ‘Hacks’ and More
item.description: Nominees for the 78th Emmy Awards were announced on Wednesday. Here’s how to watch the shows.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms (TV Program)
Love Story: John F Kennedy Jr & Carolyn Bessette (TV Program)
Only Murders in the Building (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Widow’s Bay,’ an Apple TV Breakout Hit, Scores 19 Emmy Nominations
item.description: The Apple TV horror comedy, one of the buzziest shows of the spring, has parlayed that enthusiasm into an Emmys haul, with 19 nominations.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Why ‘Heated Rivalry’ Received No Emmy Nominations While Connor Storrie Did
item.description: Even if voters had wanted to honor the gay hockey drama, which became a hit on HBO Max, they couldn’t have. But the Emmys did recognize one of the stars.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Connor Storrie, left, and Hudson Williams in “Heated Rivalry.” As a Canadian production, it was not eligible for Emmy nominations.

item.title: 2026 Emmy Nominations: ‘The Pitt’ and ‘Hacks’ Lead the Pack
item.description: The HBO Max medical series earned 25 nominations in the drama category. And in comedy, the final season of “Hacks” set a record with 24 nominations.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin

item.title: Emmy Nominees 2026: See the Full List
item.description: The 78th Emmy Awards ceremony is planned for Sept. 14. See all the nominees below.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: ‘The Hole’: Urban Apocalypse as Tuneful Chamber Drama
item.description: Tsai Ming-liang’s 1990s doomsday film gets its first theatrical run with a new 35 mm print at both Film at Lincoln Center and Metrograph.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: In Some U.K. Hospitals, the T.L.C. Includes a Movie Theater
item.description: A British nonprofit helps patients escape the stress and boredom of ward life by watching the latest blockbusters.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Dominic Whisson for The New York Times
item.media:description: “When they come here they can be themselves,” Nikki Mulhall, a nurse at Chelsea and Westminster Hospital in London, said of patients who attend the screenings.

item.title: How ‘Little House’ Welcomes Native Neighbors
item.description: The real Ingalls family built their “little house on the prairie” on Osage land and evinced racist attitudes. A new TV adaptation tries to reckon with this while preserving the story’s heart.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:description: From left, Xander Cole and Corvin Mack on horseback filming a new adaptation of “Little House on the Prairie” reshaped to include Native characters and perspectives.

item.title: How Therapist Esther Perel Helped Olivia Wilde Perfect the Sexual Dynamics in ‘The Invite’
item.description: The psychotherapist helped Olivia Wilde and her cast ground the antics of their relationship comedy in the reality of clinical practice.
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz and Rosie Marks

item.title: A Scrappy Photo Festival in Bangladesh Redefines Global Art
item.description: A critic finds in Bangladesh a subversive exhibition that has flourished during a seismic regime change.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms

item.title: Decades Late, Museum Scrambles to Return 2,700 Native Remains
item.description: The American Museum of Natural History said it would repatriate hair clippings that must be returned to tribes under a 1990 federal law.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Christopher Gregory-Rivera for The New York Times
item.media:description: Data sheets from an anthropologist who collected hair samples from Native Americans in the 1890s are stored in boxes at the American Museum of Natural History in New York.

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Geri Allen
item.description: Vernon Reid, Kassa Overall, Farah Jasmine Griffin and more artists and writers share their favorites from this pianist who connected jazz music’s past and future.
item.dc:creator: Giovanni Russonello

item.title: A New ‘Little House’ Broadens and Updates the Prairie
item.description: A Netflix adaptation of the beloved tale empowers Ma and adds new Black and Native characters to challenge prejudices. The creators know criticism is coming.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Karen Asher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s “Little House on the Prairie” was filmed in Winnipeg. The new Ingalls family includes Skywalker Hughes as Mary, left, and Alice Halsey as Laura.

item.title: Late Night Calls a Foul on Trump After His World Cup Intervention
item.description: President Trump may not have known what a red card in soccer is, Ronny Chieng said: “But you know what he did know? How to get a guy a pardon.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
